behaviors.description: A plugin whose manifest is missing a required field is rejected before any code from that plugin is executed.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The validation logic itself is Unit-testable in isolation, but the critical constraint — 'before any code is executed' — is a sequencing guarantee between the manifest validator and the module importer. A Unit test of the validation function cannot verify that the loader refrains from calling require/import on a rejected plugin. MOCK-HIDE: If we mock the module loader, we always pass trivially; a spy on require/import calls must be verified against actual loader wiring reading real manifests from a temp directory. The failure lives at the hand-off between validator and importer, making Integration the minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp plugin directory with one plugin whose manifest.json omits a required field (e.g., 'name') and one valid plugin. Instrument the invalid plugin's entry module with a side-effect (e.g., writes to a shared flag). Run the loader against the directory. Assert: (a) the loader emits a validation error for the invalid plugin, and (b) the side-effect flag is never set, proving the entry module was never imported.

behaviors.description: When plugin A declares a dependency on plugin B, plugin B's onLoad hook is called before plugin A's.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The topological sort algorithm is Unit-testable, but the behavior's failure mode is not in the sorting — it's in whether the sorted order is actually used when invoking onLoad hooks. A bug that sorts correctly but invokes in declaration order would pass a Unit test. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the hook invocations and asserting call order still requires the real dependency resolution pipeline to feed the correct order into the invoker. We need manifest files on disk, the resolver, and the hook dispatcher all wired together to see a real failure. Integration is the minimum level.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp directory with two plugins: plugin B (no dependencies) and plugin A (declares dependency on B). Each plugin's onLoad records its name to a shared invocation log. Run the loader. Assert the log shows B before A.

behaviors.description: When two plugins each declare a dependency on the other, the loader reports the cycle and loads neither.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Cycle detection (e.g., DFS on a dependency graph) is Unit-testable in isolation. However, 'loads neither' is a separate guarantee — the loader must not invoke onLoad or import entry modules for either plugin. That constraint spans the cycle detector, the load gating logic, and the hook dispatcher. A Unit test of the detection function cannot verify that the dispatch layer respects the cycle gate. REJECTION-GATE: I considered Unit for the detection algorithm but it cannot falsify 'loads neither', so Integration stands.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp directory with plugin X declaring dependency on Y, and plugin Y declaring dependency on X. Instrument both entry modules with side-effect flags. Run the loader. Assert: (a) the loader emits a cycle error naming both plugins, and (b) neither side-effect flag was set and neither onLoad was called.

behaviors.description: A plugin's onLoad hook is only invoked after the plugin's entry module has been imported and executed.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: This behavior is entirely about the sequencing between two loader phases: module import/execution and hook dispatch. There is no standalone function to Unit test — the failure only manifests when the phases are wired in the wrong order, or when onLoad is called on a module object that hasn't been initialized yet. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking require/import defeats the test — we need a real module file that sets state during its top-level execution, and the onLoad must observe that state. The failure boundary is between the importer and the hook caller. Integration is the minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Create a plugin whose entry module sets a module-level flag (e.g., `module.exports.ready = true`) during execution. The plugin's onLoad reads that flag and throws if it is not set. Run the loader. Assert onLoad completes without error, confirming the entry module was fully executed before the hook was called.

behaviors.description: When a plugin throws an error during its onLoad hook, the loader continues initializing the remaining plugins that do not depend on it.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Error recovery across multiple plugins is inherently a multi-module concern — the error handler, the load sequencer, and all subsequent plugin hooks must interact correctly. A Unit test can verify that a single try/catch swallows an error, but it cannot verify that the loader resumes the remaining queue and invokes subsequent hooks. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking subsequent plugins' hooks makes the test trivially pass regardless of whether the loader actually continues; real plugin files with observable side-effects are needed to detect a premature abort. Integration is the minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Create three plugins with no inter-dependencies: plugin A (onLoad succeeds), plugin B (onLoad throws), plugin C (onLoad records to a log). Run the loader. Assert: (a) the loader reports an error for plugin B, and (b) plugin C's onLoad was called and its log entry is present, proving the loader continued past the failure.
